message:
feat(mvcc): morsel-driven parallel INSERT scheduling primitive (AG-3B / IMPL-24 follow-up)

Adds a new `morsel_parallel_insert` submodule under `fsqlite_mvcc` that
implements the scheduling half of a morsel-driven bulk-insert path.

Background
----------
Large bulk `INSERT ... VALUES (...), (...), ...` and `INSERT ... SELECT`
statements currently serialize on a single writer thread even when the
underlying workload is trivially parallelizable: contiguous rowid
range, single root page, no FK fan-out, no triggers. MT-benchmarks
consistently show this as a dominant tail item in bulk-load
workloads.

The morsel-driven execution model from Leis et al.
("Morsel-Driven Parallelism: A NUMA-Aware Query Evaluation Framework
for the Many-Core Age", SIGMOD 2014) is the standard academic answer:
split the incoming row stream into fixed-size morsels and round-robin
them across worker threads so that each worker operates on a
contiguous, cache-friendly batch.

This commit lands the scheduling primitive **only**. It is
deliberately not wired into any execution path yet because actually
executing a morsel requires a per-worker storage cursor, FK/trigger
replay, and MVCC handoff that live elsewhere in the crate and want
their own staged commits.

Contract
--------
* `MorselScheduler::split_into_morsels(rows, start_rowid, root_page)`
  produces a `Vec<Morsel>` whose `start_rowid` ranges are contiguous
  and non-overlapping. The caller is responsible for having already
  reserved `[start_rowid, start_rowid + total_rows)` via the
  rowid allocator — the primitive does not touch the allocator.
* `MorselScheduler::schedule(morsels, worker_count)` round-robins each
  morsel to worker `i % worker_count`. Empty inputs produce empty
  outputs without allocating per-worker bins for unused workers.

Both APIs are pure data transforms with no I/O and no global state,
which keeps them trivially testable and safe to call from any thread.
They do not borrow from the caller-supplied row stream except to
copy `SqliteValue`s into the owned morsel buffer, so handoff to a
rayon scope / tokio spawn is straightforward.

Why stop at scheduling
----------------------
The per-worker execution side wants its own design discussion:
* which cursor implementation to hand each worker (direct pager vs.
  the fast-path scan cursor vs. a new morsel-local cursor variant);
* how to replay FK/trigger side effects in order while executing
  the morsels out-of-order for throughput;
* how to commit the combined result under the existing MVCC
  coordinator without double-counting rowids.

Each of those decisions is independently controversial and warrants
its own commit with its own review. Landing the scheduler now means
the unit-test surface exists (rowid assignment, round-robin
distribution, empty-input handling) for future commits to rely on
without re-litigating the primitive.

Also registered the module in `crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/lib.rs` so
downstream crates (e.g. fsqlite-executor) can consume the types
without a separate refactor.

No public re-exports beyond the module registration; the two types
(`Morsel`, `MorselScheduler`) are discoverable via
`fsqlite_mvcc::morsel_parallel_insert::{Morsel, MorselScheduler}`.
